channel.title: Midtown East on Gourmand Syndrome
channel.description: Recent content in Midtown East on Gourmand Syndrome

item.title: Empire Steak House
item.description: Summary We were looking for a decent steak place that was walkable from where we were. The best steak house in my opinion in the city is Keen’s Steakhouse, so expectations were set pretty low. This venue is fairly nice. It’s close to Smith and Wollensky and located inside some hotel. This was one of the few steakhouses at this time that had hardly any people, so one can only think that this place wouldn’t be that good.

item.title: Nonono
item.description: Summary The people behind My Name is Han opened a cool Yakitori place. On a Saturday, it was easy to get a table for 2. The zuke maguro is marinated tuna sashimi with fresh jalapeno sauce. The tuna was actually pretty good, and the jalapeno sauce has a nice kick to it. For the yakitoris: The skirt with shishito was pretty good. There wasn’t anything super special about it, but I really liked the flavor of the coal they use here.

item.title: Bap
item.description: Summary The food here was surprisingly decent. I usually don’t expect many places in Midtown East to be good, let alone Asian ethnic foods. We ordered a pineapple Mocktail and a glass of Hite, which is nothing to write home about. The banchan here was pretty good, although portion size is on the smaller side. You can ask for additional banchan as needed. Here you got kimchi, mixed salad, jalapenos, pickled cauliflower, pickled daikon cubes, and pickled daikon slices.

item.title: Paname
item.description: Summary I’m not a fan of brunch at all, so this was the first restaurant in the area I was in that seemed interesting to me. I completely forgot it was Easter Sunday, but luckily their prixe fix menu had all the regulars on it. The bread was nothing to write home about. It came slightly on the cold side. The escargots de bourgogne in baby potatoes were actually pretty good.

item.title: Kung Fu Little Steamed Buns
item.description: Good XLB. Was flavorful, BUT the price is pretty expensive for what you get. The dessert XLB filled with nutella/banana was gimmicky but tasted good.

item.title: Baked by Melissa
item.description: These are super tiny one bite mini cupcakes. Pretty good though the flavor profiles aren’t that different from each other.

item.title: Strip House
item.description: Steak was just OK. Service was slow as hell. Truffle cream of spinach was good.

item.title: American Cut
item.description: Decent for the price. This is Marc Forgione’s steak house.

item.title: 212 Steakhouse
item.description: Ok, this place is the only legit place in NYC that has real kobe certified beef from Japan. It was delicious and cheap… with the groupon.

item.title: Grand Central Oyster Bar
item.description: Pretty decent. Worth this 1 time trip and that’s about it. The oyster pan roast was alright. The soft shell crab was pretty decent too. I think I’d get the clam chowder over the oyster pan roast.

item.title: Momosan Ramen
item.description: It’s ok. Broth is not great, but tuna bowl is good. I think this restaurant is owned by Morimoto.

item.title: Macchina
item.description: They are known on Instagram for their Burrata pizza which was about $25. It was ok. I wouldn’t go out of my way to get it because it literally is just a margarita style pizza with a big piece of burrata cheese in the middle. The burrata wasn’t that soft when cut for some reason.

item.title: Fine and Rare
item.description: Jazzy loungy restaurant. Might be good for a date spot if you’re into dine and jazz, but the food was just OK.

item.title: Her Name Is Han
item.description: Pretty good Korean food. Fairly priced and family style. They have BOSSAM, and it was really good.

item.title: Tonkatsu Matsunoya
item.description: This place is pretty bomb and NO TIP NEEDED! Would come back here again. The crust was really good.

item.title: Cafe Zaiya
item.description: Decent black sesame bubble milk tea with red bean dessert bakery stuff.
